_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve graphs Treasury yields across maturities from 1 month to 30 years, with an upward slope indicating higher yields for longer durations, reflecting normal economic expectations. An inverted curve, where short-term yields exceed long-term yields, has historically preceded every modern U.S. recession by 6 to 18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.58% (maturing June 2026), and the 2s10s spread—the difference between the 10-year and 2-year yields—is positive at 0.42 points. This contrasts with the period from 2022 to 2024 when the curve was inverted (spread below 0.0 points), indicating a re-steepening above zero since then.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread serves as a key recession indicator, where inversion (spread below zero) historically signals economic contraction risk within 6-18 months. The yield curve was inverted through 2022-2024 but has since 'un-inverted' and re-steepened to 0.42 points, suggesting a reduced immediate recession risk relative to the prior inversion period. The New York Fed recession probability currently stands at 31%, reflecting moderate recession risk consistent with the curve's recent steepening. However, since the curve often un-inverts shortly before a recession's onset, the absence of inversion does not definitively preclude a recession.
notes.now: The yield curve's current upward slope, with a positive 2s10s spread of 0.42 points, suggests reduced recession risk compared to recent inverted periods. The moderate 31% recession probability from the NY Fed complements this view, indicating some concern without signaling an imminent downturn. The un-inversion of the curve denotes a shift in market expectations but should be interpreted cautiously alongside other data.
